title: Quickselect (Kth Largest)
code: public static int FindKthLargest(int[] nums, int k)
int p = Partition(nums, lo, hi, pivotIndex);
private static int Partition(int[] a, int lo, int hi, int pivotIndex)
for (int i = lo; i < hi; i++)

frames.caption: Find the 2nd largest of [3, 2, 1, 5, 6, 4]. Quickselect partitions like quicksort but recurses into only ONE side.
frames.note: Quickselect is divide-and-conquer with a twist: it DIVIDES by partitioning around a pivot, but instead of conquering BOTH sides (like quicksort/merge sort) it conquers only the side containing the answer. That single change drops the expected time from O(n log n) to O(n).

frames.caption: target = n - k = 6 - 2 = 4. The value that ends up at index 4 (ascending) is the 2nd largest.
frames.note: For "k-th largest" we convert to an ascending-order index: the k-th largest value is the one that would sit at index n - k after a full sort. Here n = 6 and k = 2, so target = 4 — we never sort the whole array, we just keep partitioning until SOME pivot lands exactly on index 4. The value there is the answer.

frames.caption: Active range a[0..5]. Choose pivot = a[5] = 4; partition this range around it.
frames.note: The reviewer picks a RANDOM index in [lo, hi] so adversarial inputs cannot force the O(n^2) worst case. We fix pivotIndex = hi here only to make the animation reproducible; the partition logic is identical either way.

frames.caption: Park pivot 4 at a[5]; set store = 0. Everything left of store will be < pivot.
frames.note: Lomuto partition starts by parking the chosen pivot at the end of the active range (swap it to a[hi]) so the forward scan has a clean run of unknown elements to its left. The "store" pointer marks the boundary of the region known to be < pivot; it begins at lo because nothing is confirmed smaller yet.

frames.caption: a[0] = 3 < pivot 4: already at store 0, store -> 1.
frames.note: The scanning element a[i] is LESS than the pivot, so it belongs in the left (smaller) region. We swap it into the store slot and advance store. This grows the < pivot prefix by one. Each element is touched exactly once in this pass, so partitioning a range of size m costs O(m) — the linear work at every level.

frames.caption: a[1] = 2 < pivot 4: already at store 1, store -> 2.
frames.note: The scanning element a[i] is LESS than the pivot, so it belongs in the left (smaller) region. We swap it into the store slot and advance store. This grows the < pivot prefix by one. Each element is touched exactly once in this pass, so partitioning a range of size m costs O(m) — the linear work at every level.

frames.caption: a[2] = 1 < pivot 4: already at store 2, store -> 3.
frames.note: The scanning element a[i] is LESS than the pivot, so it belongs in the left (smaller) region. We swap it into the store slot and advance store. This grows the < pivot prefix by one. Each element is touched exactly once in this pass, so partitioning a range of size m costs O(m) — the linear work at every level.

frames.caption: a[3] = 5 >= pivot 4: leave it in the right region, just advance i.
frames.note: The scanning element a[i] is >= the pivot, so it already belongs in the right region — leave it where it is and just advance i. Only elements strictly smaller than the pivot trigger a swap into the store boundary; everything else stays put.

frames.caption: a[4] = 6 >= pivot 4: leave it in the right region, just advance i.
frames.note: The scanning element a[i] is >= the pivot, so it already belongs in the right region — leave it where it is and just advance i. Only elements strictly smaller than the pivot trigger a swap into the store boundary; everything else stays put.

frames.caption: Place pivot 4 at its final index p = 3. Left of 3 is < 4, right is >= 4.
frames.note: The scan is done: everything left of store is < pivot, everything from store..hi-1 is >= pivot. Swapping the parked pivot at a[hi] into a[store] drops it into its FINAL sorted position p = store. That is the key invariant — after one partition, the pivot is exactly where it would be in the sorted array, even though neither side is sorted yet.

frames.caption: p (3) < target (4): the 2nd largest is to the RIGHT. Discard a[..3], recurse on a[4..5].
frames.note: The pivot landed at p < target, so the value we want sits to its RIGHT (it is larger). Discard the entire left side including the pivot by setting lo = p + 1 and loop again on just the right partition. Throwing away one whole side every round is what collapses the expected cost to O(n).

frames.caption: Active range a[4..5]. Choose pivot = a[5] = 5; partition this range around it.
frames.note: The reviewer picks a RANDOM index in [lo, hi] so adversarial inputs cannot force the O(n^2) worst case. We fix pivotIndex = hi here only to make the animation reproducible; the partition logic is identical either way.

frames.caption: Park pivot 5 at a[5]; set store = 4. Everything left of store will be < pivot.
frames.note: Lomuto partition starts by parking the chosen pivot at the end of the active range (swap it to a[hi]) so the forward scan has a clean run of unknown elements to its left. The "store" pointer marks the boundary of the region known to be < pivot; it begins at lo because nothing is confirmed smaller yet.

frames.caption: a[4] = 6 >= pivot 5: leave it in the right region, just advance i.
frames.note: The scanning element a[i] is >= the pivot, so it already belongs in the right region — leave it where it is and just advance i. Only elements strictly smaller than the pivot trigger a swap into the store boundary; everything else stays put.

frames.caption: Place pivot 5 at its final index p = 4. Left of 4 is < 5, right is >= 5.
frames.note: The scan is done: everything left of store is < pivot, everything from store..hi-1 is >= pivot. Swapping the parked pivot at a[hi] into a[store] drops it into its FINAL sorted position p = store. That is the key invariant — after one partition, the pivot is exactly where it would be in the sorted array, even though neither side is sorted yet.

frames.caption: p == target (4) -> a[4] = 5 is the 2nd largest. Done.
frames.note: The pivot landed on index p == target — by the invariant it is in its final sorted spot, so a[p] is the k-th largest and we are done. No further recursion: we got lucky (or rather, the one-sided search converged) right on the target index.

frames.caption: The 2nd largest of [3, 2, 1, 4, 5, 6] (now partly partitioned) is 5, found at index 4.
frames.note: Quickselect found the k-th largest by repeatedly partitioning and keeping only the side holding target, never sorting the rest. Expected O(n) time (geometric n + n/2 + n/4 + ... = 2n), O(1) extra space, but worst case O(n^2) on consistently bad pivots — which a RANDOM pivot (as in the reviewer) makes astronomically unlikely.
